briefing.opening: Good morning. Welcome to the Daily Climate Briefing for July 24, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Crisis Situation
sections.content: The global climate crisis is intensifying, leading to ongoing emergencies such as extreme heatwaves, droughts, and wildfires [7, 32, 104, 105]. Domestically, longer summers have been observed, with heatwaves and tropical nights starting earlier than usual and persisting until September. Over the past decade, heatwaves have increased 2.2 times and tropical nights 3 times [20, 22, 31, 34, 40]. Particularly in Europe, 'flash droughts' coupled with large-scale wildfires have led to the evacuation of tens of thousands of people. Warnings of a 'super El Niño' suggest that 2027 could be the hottest year on record, alongside reports of rising sea temperatures in the Galapagos Islands [27, 32, 104, 105, 106]. These climate changes are causing unprecedented disruption to ecosystems, including a 52% surge in methane emissions from rice paddies [12, 97], and are escalating into widespread socioeconomic problems such as increased antibiotic resistance in pathogens [96] and threats to indigenous nations' lands [93]. The UN has warned that conflict and climate change threaten progress in eradicating global hunger and has announced the potential addition of endangered heritage sites to its list [84, 95].

sections.title: ⚠️ Energy Transition Policies and Economic Challenges
sections.content: Amid deepening discussions surrounding energy transition policies, Climate Minister Kim Seong-hwan warned that expanding renewable energy alone could lead to soaring electricity prices and damage industrial competitiveness [53, 54, 57, 58, 60, 66]. The Minister stated that nuclear power and renewable energy must be utilized together to meet increasing electricity demand, and that sufficient power for the Honam semiconductor industry can be supplied through a mix of existing nuclear power and renewables [55, 61, 64, 72, 73, 78, 80]. However, despite renewable energy being projected to become the largest power source this year, questions are being raised about the potential for carbon emissions in the power sector to reach record highs, and the issue of cost burden associated with renewable energy expansion is also being discussed [59, 62]. The international community is moving to formalize legal obligations for climate action, emphasizing the need for Korea to reform its judicial system regarding fossil fuel issues [23].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Climate Change Response Efforts and Innovation
sections.content: Domestic and international efforts and innovative attempts to address climate change are continuing. Domestically, achievements in international cooperation and technological development are notable, including the development of 'Saryopi,' a climate change-tailored forage crop [1], and the completion of a three-year training program by the Korea Rural Community Corporation to strengthen Guatemala's climate change response capabilities [2, 3, 4, 15, 17, 28, 37]. Furthermore, the Wonju Climate Change Promotion Center is raising awareness about the value of moss as a carbon sink through its special exhibition, 'Net-Zero with Moss' [5, 9, 11, 19, 26, 30, 33], and Namwon City has completed maintenance of its sewage facilities in preparation for frequent heavy rainfall [21, 35]. The Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy is participating in discussions on new trade norms through a public hearing for the 'Green Economy Agreement,' responding to trade barriers caused by climate change [6, 16, 36]. The Ministry of Oceans and Fisheries' amendment to the 「Aquaculture Industry Development Act」 has laid the groundwork for adapting to climate change and supporting aquaculture farmers' self-generation facilities for renewable energy [18, 24, 75]. In addition, active movements for carbon neutrality are underway in various sectors, including cooperation for carbon neutrality in the textile industry [43], the launch of Siheung City's Climate Citizen Network [50], and the establishment of a renewable energy circular ecosystem through recycling waste batteries and panels [71, 79] [41, 44, 45, 46, 48, 49, 51, 52].

sections.title: 🌱 Renewable Energy Infrastructure Development and Future Outlook
sections.content: Positive news regarding renewable energy infrastructure development continues both domestically and internationally. Solar and wind power generation in the ASEAN region is projected to show less than a 1% change by 2030 despite climate change [10], and international efforts to expand renewable energy are becoming visible, with China aiming to increase its renewable energy consumption by 53% by 2030 [65]. Domestically, there is growing recognition that water energy will play a key role in stabilizing the power grid in the era of renewable energy, with pumped hydro, tidal, and hydrothermal energy emerging as carbon-neutral and RE100 solutions [56, 67, 69]. Particularly, the 'power grid across the sea' is credited with paving the way for renewable energy expansion in the Southwest region [68], and Yeonggwang-gun's public-interest solar project has received government investment approval, putting it on track [76]. Private sector participation and achievements are also prominent, including Samsung Life's new investment in a renewable energy fund [77] and expectations for SK E&S to expand its market share in the renewable energy PPA market [81].

sections.title: 🌍 Complex Understanding of Climate Change and Social Discourse
sections.content: Climate change is no longer perceived as a simple environmental issue but as a complex challenge spanning the entire economy and society [98, 99]. Analyses showing the unprecedented vulnerability of the Spanish coastline due to climate change [83] and research on its impact on Arctic marine life [85] illustrate changes in ecosystems. An ecologist's perspective that climate change is rewriting nature's rules [90] reminds us of the gravity of the situation we face. Meanwhile, diverse perspectives and social discourse on climate change are emerging, such as views on whether mainstream media exaggerates the threat of climate change [91] or research findings that recycling alone cannot stop climate change [92]. Domestically, active discussions on policy direction are underway [25], including opinions that a 'breather' is sometimes needed in the climate change marathon [13] and criticisms that the amendment to the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act shifts an excessive burden onto future generations [42].

briefing.closing: This concludes the Daily Climate Briefing for July 24, 2026. Based on an understanding of the complex impacts of climate change, comprehensive response efforts for a sustainable future are becoming increasingly vital.

articles.title: What can the Galápagos Islands’ animals teach us about the threats of El Niño?
articles.description: In this week’s newsletter: the archipelago at the centre of the extreme weather phenomenon could help scientists predict how other marine ecosystems will adapt to our warming climate• Don’t get Down to Earth delivered to your inbox? Sign up hereThe ocean waters around the Galápagos Islands are hot. Very hot. Observed for centuries in the Pacific by fishers, the El Niño weather phenomenon begins when the prevailing trade winds slow, allowing warm water to spread east along the coast of South Amer

summary.phenomenon: Ocean temperatures around the Galápagos Islands are exceptionally high. This condition is part of the El Niño weather phenomenon, which has been observed by Pacific fishers for centuries.
summary.cause: The El Niño phenomenon is triggered when prevailing trade winds weaken, enabling warm ocean waters to spread eastward along the coast of South America.
summary.outlook: The fauna of the Galápagos Islands could provide scientists with insights into how other marine ecosystems may adapt to a warming climate.
